reason: Both answers hit all key points (existential risk, owned-channel capture, diversification, fast-detect/repair) with similar structure and near-identical rule-of-thumb conclusions. B is slightly stronger on technical correctness and specificity: it correctly flags that remote-config must stay data-only since remote code execution would violate Chrome Web Store policy (a real pitfall A's "remote-config the selectors" recommendation doesn't guard against), and it adds accurate, concrete details (ytd-* custom element tags as stable anchors, SPA navigation behavior favoring MutationObservers) that show deeper domain grounding. A is also solid but slightly more generic and slightly overstates ToS/takedown risk as "bigger" than DOM fragility without strong justification, whereas B's framing (fragility as the dominant, near-certain risk) fits the question better.
